feed.title: Combined summary - Q-Lock: Quantum-Resistant Spending via ECDSA + Hash-Based Secrets

entry.title: Combined summary - Q-Lock: Quantum-Resistant Spending via ECDSA + Hash-Based Secrets
entry.summary: The **Q-Lock: Quantum-Resistant Spending Protocol** has been put forward as a new methodology to enhance the security of Bitcoin transactions against potential quantum computing threats without altering the existing cryptographic base of ECDSA. This protocol integrates an additional hash-based secret layer, utilizing SHA256 and Merkle trees, to secure transactions through a two-phase commit-reveal scheme. This approach aims to prevent quantum computers from reverse-engineering SHA256, thereby securing the transaction outputs. The Q-Lock protocol maintains transaction sizes similar to those of FALCON signatures at approximately 3 KB and supports integration through soft forks and BIP-32 HD wallets. It proposes a novel address format that includes witness versioning and Bech32m encoding, aiming for improved security and efficiency. In its operation, Q-Lock requires users to generate a standard ECDSA keypair accompanied by 64 random secrets, forming a commitment array that is structured into a Merkle tree. The Merkle root, combined with the public key hash, is stored on-chain, while the secrets and their corresponding tree structures are kept within the user's wallet. To conduct a transaction, the protocol first locks the transaction outputs without revealing the public key, followed by a reveal phase where the public key and selected secrets are disclosed based on the block hash at the time of the transaction. This process ensures that only the rightful owner can finalize the transaction, effectively safeguarding against quantum decryption attempts. Furthermore, the discussion brings attention to BIP-360 and SHRINCS, highlighting that the former does not introduce new post-quantum algorithms but rather adds a new Taproot address that disables the implied key spend path, which is essential for any post-quantum solution. Without such a mechanism, the system remains vulnerable. On the other hand, SHRINCS is presented as a simpler and more efficient alternative, requiring only one OP code and producing 324-byte signatures. SHRINCS solely relies on hashes for security, thus reducing potential attack surfaces compared to the more complex Q-Lock proposal. This comparison suggests a need for reconsideration of the Q-Lock approach in favor of simpler, more effective solutions like SHRINCS, emphasizing the importance of re-evaluating BIP-360 as a foundational requirement for post-quantum security measures in Bitcoin.
